title: Skin or Skim? Inside Investment and Hedge Fund Performance
abstract: Hedge fund managers contribute substantial personal capital, or “skin in the game,” into their funds. Although these allocations may better align incentives, managers may also strategically allocate their private capital in ways that negatively affect outside investors. We find that funds with more inside investment outperform other funds within the same family. This relationship is driven by managerial decisions to invest their own capital in their least-scalable strategies and restrict the entry of new outsider capital into these funds. Our results suggest that insider capital may work as a rent-extraction mechanism at the expense of fund participation of outside investors.

author.Arpit Gupta: Leonard N. Stern School of Business, New York University, New York, New York 10012
author.Kunal Sachdeva: Stephen M. Ross School of Business, University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, Michigan 48109

article.1. Introduction: Although delegated asset managers are commonly seen as being compensated through fees imposed on outside investors, insider funding is an important, and previously overlooked component of managerial earnings. Of a total $3 trillion in assets under management by hedge funds, $400 billion can be attributed to investments from insiders and related parties.1The discretion by General Partner (GP) to commit capital to their investment strategies has raised concerns by investors and regulators.2In this paper, we explore the potential for this large allocation of this inside investment, or “skin in the game,” to affect managerial incentives regarding strategy allocation, signaling ability to outsiders, and capital raising in ways that impact returns received by outside investors and their ability to access funds. We argue that the provision of internal capital can influence hedge fund management in two ways. First, we use aBerk and Green (2004)model of asset management to show how managers can scale funds differently based on the presence of insider capital. When managing personal capital, managers internalize the fact that raising additional outside capital is dilutive in the sense that it causes the strategy to operate further from its optimum size, lowering the returns for all existing investors. Second, even when managers may have other strategies to manage dilution costs, insider capital may still be preferred because it does not withdraw at inopportune times (Shleifer and Vishny 1997) and does not expose managers to reveal information to outsiders (Glode and Green 2011). Under both explanations, greater inside investment will induce managers to limit the size of their funds, resulting in higher alpha even in equilibrium for participating investors but at the cost of capital participation by outside investors. As a result, managers at the top end of the income distribution will see excess returns, while other classes of limited partners—such as public pension funds, endowments, and foundations—will systematically underperform. Guided by these insights, we examine the relationship between inside investment, fund returns, and fund flows through a novel use of a comprehensive data set, Form ADV, provided by the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Testing the model’s predictions, we find that hedge fund managers substantially outperform in their private investments through rationing fund access to outside investors. We provide four key pieces of supporting evidence. First, we show that insider capital is deployed to less-scalable funds—those with larger market impact. Funds with more insider capital are smaller: one additional percent of inside investment is associated with a fund that is smaller by $7–$10 million. This result holds both unconditionally andwithinfirm.3We also test the relationship between inside investment and fund scalability. Following the methodology used byYin (2016), which measures the decreasing return to scale by regressing style-adjusted returns against lagged assets, we find that funds with greater insider investment are also less scalable. Insider capital also appears to be a more stable source of capital for hedge funds facing poor returns, suggesting that inside capital may help alleviate run risk for hedge funds. Second, we find that funds with greater insider capital are more likely to restrict outsider capital entry on both intensive and extensive margins. On the intensive margin, managers limit outsider inflows to the high-performing insider funds. On the extensive margin, insiders can strategically restrict access by closing a fund to outsiders entirely. The presence of funds that are closed to outside investors is a challenge to reconcile for conventional models of delegated asset management, as managers are not capturing the entire surplus by forgoing the management fees earned on additional capital. Instead, we find that such funds strongly outperform, delivering 2.8% more excess returns yearly. Such strong performance suggests that outside investors are in fact rationed from fund participation. Notably, such funds closed to outside investors are disproportionately funded by inside capital. Third, we examine the returns and characteristics of portfolios sorted by their percentage of inside investment. We find that high inside investment funds perform better than low inside investment funds, but they appear to take on higher amounts of tail risks. Consistent with this explanation, we find that high inside investment funds appear to offer capital stability, especially after experiencing recent underperformance. Inside investors, in other words, are less run-prone and provide additional insurance to fund managers that enables them to pursue alpha-generation strategies which come with some tail risks. Fourth, we compute excess returns on a risk-adjusted basis and find that inside investment remains an important predictor of excess returns. This relationship holds when comparing different funds within the same firm and time period and within the same fund over time. Our panel regression controls for the seven factors inFung and Hsieh (2004)at the fund level. An increase in inside investment by a standard deviation within the same firm and month is associated with annualized excess returns of 0.9 percentage points. We confirm this finding in an event study that focuses on firms that create a second fund within the same firm. In this event study, we find that the original fund tend outperforms when inside capital remains in the original fund, relative to when insiders reallocate their capital to the newly formed fund. These results suggest the possibility of “skimming” motives on the part of fund managers. We rule out natural alternate explanations for our results. A chief concern is the signaling issues associated with insider capital. We find that funds with greater insider capital accept less outside capital—including other funds that are part of their families—which is inconsistent with a story that emphasizes the role of inside investment as a signaling tool or marketing strategy. We also carefully rule out a variety of other stories. We account for a numerous other alternative risks (including macroeconomic, systematic, and tail risks); include additional controls such as for share restrictions; and consider a variety of alternative measures of inside investment. In all tests, we find that our results are robust to these numerous controls. In light of these findings, our results also come with several important caveats. Although we establish inside investment as an important predictor of excess returns and highlight the role for the strategic allocation and capacity constraints in understanding these results, it is possible that other channels operate in addition to the ones we emphasize. It is possible that inside investors are better informed about the skill of various fund managers, in ways that differ from fund scaling, and that they allocate capital accordingly. Alternatively, high insider-investment funds may be less subject to agency conflicts and engage in superior research analysis (Berk et al. 2017). Also, it is possible that higher returns from high insider-investment funds are a proxy for some risk factors, unrelated to theFung and Hsieh (2004)factors. Although we cannot fully rule out the relationship between inside investment and other fund attributes, understanding inside investment through the lens of fund capacity constraints is most consistent with our results. Our paper provides novel evidence to the literature examining fund families, showing that hedge fund managers allocate personal capital strategically across funds at the expense of crowding out outsider capital.4Our key contribution is to document how hedge fund managers prioritize personal investments to less-scalable strategies, and subsequently restrict the size of their funds, in ways to boost performance. This result connects closely withYin (2016), who emphasizes the role of decreasing returns to scale among hedge funds, which managers seem not to fully internalize given their stated contract terms including management and performance fees. We document that a nonstated contract term and the presence of insider capital provides an important additional reason for fund managers to operate their funds at smaller scales. The impact of these personal stakes on outside investors is mixed: some investors are able to coinvest with insiders and earn superior returns due to smaller fund sizes and the alignment of interests. However, the smaller scale of insider funds can have detrimental consequences on other outside investors, who are rationed out of fund participation. We also contribute to research on managerial ownership in the hedge fund industry. Based on the assumption that fee income is reinvested in funds,Agarwal et al. (2009)finds a positive relationship between estimated managerial investment and subsequent fund performance. We extend this result and provide additional context by finding evidence for strategic allocation of private capital, as opposed to personal stakes which are increasing mechanically, and by also establishing one key mechanism for the outperformance of insider funds: the choice of managers to limit outside capital investment on funds with greater private capital.5 More broadly, our core results advances knowledge of the asset management industry by highlighting the role for inside investment as an incentive to alter managers’ decisions to strategically allocate capital in ways that affect the returns and investment opportunities available to institutional investors. Furthermore, this paper contributes to the literature on managerial earnings by emphasizing the unique role for returns on personal capital contributions as a component of overall managerial income in the hedge fund industry.6Especially related isOzik and Sadka (2015), who highlight how share restrictions can adversely induce information asymmetry between managers and their clients about future fund flows. We control for share restrictions throughout to highlight our contribution relative to this work. We additionally add to the literature on diseconomies of scale by highlighting the novel incentives for more optimal fund scaling generated by noncontract features of managerial behavior in the form of private capital stakes.7
article.2. Model of Inside Investment and Invest: 2.1. Managerial Discretion over Internal Capital Allocation The role of managerial discretion over internal capital allocation across funds can be seen most clearly in the case of Renaissance Technologies.8The company’s Medallion Fund is one of the most successful funds in history. However, the Medallion Fund is predominately a fund available only for insider investment (as we confirm inFigure 1). News accounts of Renaissance Technologies emphasize how the company prioritizes strategies with greater excess returns and lower scalability in the Medallion Fund while shifting strategies with lower return profiles (for reasons of scalability or staleness in execution) to other funds in the family characterized by greater outside investor participation and lower fees. Given the sorting of capital and strategies between funds within Renaissance Technologies, it is clear that there are important implications on returns earned by all investors. First, the investment opportunities marketed and available to outside institutional investors are more likely to underperform. Although, in contrast, the returns to insider funds are expected to outperform. These would also predict that the earnings from the returns to private capital would also play an important role for insiders. This role of privately invested capital is not just limited to Renaissance Technologies. The returns on privately invested capital are roughly as large for hedge fund managers as either management or performance fees (Figure 2), suggesting a tradeoff between the choice of earning returns on private capital against fee revenue levied on outside investors. When choosing where to allocate personal capital, we expect that managers will internalize the fact that raising additional capital dilutes the fund’s return due to decreasing returns to scale. 2.2. Model for Inside Investment We fix ideas by writing a simple model of capital allocation. A full exposition of our model and implications can be found in Online Appendix A. For clarity, we consider a rational, two-period, partial equilibrium model that highlights how the internal capital allocation decisions of fund managers interact with measured performance. Our simple model has several salient features that differ from previous works. First, we disaggregate capital from insiders and outsiders. This captures the idea that insiders compensation is tied to both management fees earned on outside capital and returns on insider capital. Second, we also model for endogenous fund generation in the form of multiple investment strategies and managerial discretion to differentially allocate insider capital across these strategies. Similar to other models, we model the cost of asset allocation as convex in gross returns, as this helps match stylized facts we observe in the data. The basic intuition behind our model is that managers trade off earnings through fee income on delegated asset management and returns on privately invested capital. Third, managers cannot credibly commit to not increasing the size of their fund in the future to the point that the excess returns to investment strategies are driven down to zero. This information friction is similar to a moral hazard channel. Personal capital commitments better align the incentives of managers and outsiders, providing greater incentives for managers to scale their funds less aggressively in a manner that results in greater returns to investors—but at the cost of capital participation by other outside investors. Fourth, managers have a preference for insider capital relative to outside capital. This disutility is born directly by insiders. Managers may prefer to take capital from insiders for several reasons. First, dealing with outside investors entails considerable expense. Advertising to clients on the basis of trust, experience, and dependability may enhance capital raising (Gennaioli et al. 2015) but may also entail significant marketing expenses and otherwise dilute attention to researching trading strategies. Second, delegated asset managers may have to reveal certain aspects of their strategies to credibly convince outsiders of their alpha-generation strategies, which may adversely impact the market awareness and competitiveness of the strategy (Glode and Green 2011). Finally, a more risk-based story is that inside investors may be reluctant to withdraw capital in bad times for the fund, providing additional capital stability (Shleifer and Vishny 1997,Lewis 2011). We capture these preferences for inside capital with a reduced-form cost parameter (γ) that applies to outside capital but is only borne by managers. Finally, we include the realistic possibility that managers can invest the fund’s capital in both active and passive strategies. This is captured by the investment parameter (ω), with1−𝜔1−ωrepresenting the fraction invested in the passive strategy. As insiders allocate more to the passive strategy, they would reduce the disutility of managing money and impact on the strategy, but at the expense of allocating less of the capital toward the alpha strategy. Taken together, managers in our model maximize total earnings that consists of three parts. First, managers earn profits in investing in their own strategy; second, they earn fee income on outside investors; and finally, they face a disutility from managing outsider capital. They do so subject to the participation constraint of outside investors, whose after-fee returns must exceed a benchmark return: In deciding their strategy, managers also index a fraction(1−𝜔)(1−ω)of the strategy to a passive benchmark portfolio. The baseline model assumes that fees (f) and the indexing ratio(𝜔)(ω)are exogenous. This corresponds to the value add for the insider of 2.3. Model Predictions Incorporating Inside Investment The two key frictions in our model—limited commitment and preferences for inside capital—generate clear tradeoffs in our model between inside investment, fund performance, and scale. We use our model setup to establish two key propositions, which we then take to the data. Increases in insider capital result in decreases in total capital. See Online Appendix A. □ This first result provides a natural test: high inside investment funds should be scaled to a more optimal size to take advantage of strategies that face capacity constraints. This draws from the point inGetmansky (2012)that illiquid hedge funds have more market impact and are more likely to exhibit size behavior. We test specifically whether high insider funds are even more likely to internalize market impact in their size decision. Gross returns increase in inside investment. See Online Appendix A. □ Using our model framework, we explore four key implications that establish the relationship between inside investment and fund operations and performance: Funds with a greater percentage of inside investment are in smaller, less-scalable strategies, as they are further from their capacity constraint. Funds with more inside investment are more likely to restrict capital entry by outside investors. Inside investment is more stable (i.e., less sensitive to flows). Because they are operated further from their capacity constraint, funds with greater inside capital outperform on a risk-adjusted basis. Taken together, our model predicts that managers will realize excess returns through the capital exclusion of outside investors. As a result, insiders (corresponding to fund managers) will see higher returns and greater wealth accumulation than outsiders (limited partners who invest in these fund vehicles, and represent investment by the general public). As we discuss in Online Appendix A, these results are robust to natural robustness concerns such as endogenous fund formation, alternative fee structures and indexing. We do not model fees as dynamic in our base case, based on empirical evidence that fees appear to be static, but we also theoretically consider endogenous fees.
article.3. Data: Our data set combines regulatory Form ADV filings with commercial hedge fund return series from HFR, eVestment, BarclayHedge, Eurekahedge, and CISDM. Form ADV is a required regulatory disclosure form used to register with both the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and state securities authorities. Reporting under Form ADV is governed by the U.S. Investment Advisers Act of 1940, as amended by Dodd-Frank. Disclosure requirements under this form have changed over the years. In the period from 1996 to 2011, funds with assets under management below $25 million, or fewer than 15 clients, were generally exempt from registration. Hedge funds in this period frequently used complex fund structures to evade disclosure even when assets were above this threshold. Private fund reporting increased in 2005, when the SEC went to court to force funds to count all investors as clients. Though courts ultimately struck down the SEC’s interpretation, disclosure through Form ADV increased throughout this period. Our primary sample is formed after 2011, in the aftermath of changes in required disclosure imposed by Dodd-Frank. Under prevailing regulations, all investment advisors—including hedge funds—are now required to file a Form ADV with the SEC if they (1) reach a $100 million threshold for assets under management for a typical fund, (2) reach a $150 million threshold if the firm has only private clients, and (3) have more than $25 million in assets and are not subject to examination in their home states (states that do not require examination currently include New York and Wyoming). Subsequent to their initial filing, firms must refile once a year (as long as their assets under management exceed $25 million), or if there have been changes in material information since the last filing. We link information from Form ADV from the SEC over the period 2011–2016 together with information on hedge fund returns obtained from a combination of five data sets: HFR, eVestment, BarclayHedge, Eurekahedge, and CISDM. We begin the merge with HFR, eVestment and BarclayHedge, which contain SEC identifiers for most firms, which is common to both the commercial hedge fund data sets and Form ADV. If we do not find an SEC identifier, we next look for close matches (selecting only perfect matches) among firm and fund names in both data sets, after eliminating extraneous stop words and abbreviations. In 2012, Form ADV was updated to include questions about the internal investment of funds. We draw specifically on Section 7.B.(1), question 14 of Form ADV: “What is the approximate percentage of theprivate fundbeneficially owned by you and yourrelated persons.” This question asks funds to disclose the percentage of investment stakes in the fund which can be attributed in ultimate ownership to “related persons.” Table 1shows basic summary information about our core Form ADV data set taken from 2016. The broad ADV sample is able to establish key statistics about the overall size and scope of the entire hedge fund industry beyond prior work. Table 1.Summary Statistics: ADV Data Table 1.Summary Statistics: ADV Data Notes.This summary table describes data on investment advisors taken from Form ADV in 2016. Data are only taken from funds which are registered as hedge funds; firms must have at least one hedge fund and a minimum level of assets of $20 million. Panel A describes firm level information at the level of the management company. Panel B describes information available at the level of individual funds. Some assets may be double-counted due to the inclusion of fund of funds. Inside Investment corresponds to ownership by management or related parties, the key variable explored in this paper. In this study, we take seriously the important data limitations associated with commercial hedge fund return series. As has been noted in prior research, funds retain discretion on reporting their returns (Bollen and Pool 2008,2009;Agarwal et al. 2013;Jorion and Schwarz 2014;Patton et al. 2015). We restrict our sample to funds for which we have at least 24 months of data, excluding the first 24 months of data to avoid incubation bias, and we also require funds to have at least $20 million in gross asset value. We exclude fund-of-funds because their inside investment is relatively limited, and the scope for investment is radically different.9In further robustness tests, we implement the procedure outlined inJorion and Schwarz (2019)to further address concerns of a backfill bias. Figure 3demonstrates our merge rate across the range of fund ownership. We find that funds with complete inside investment (100%) and no inside investment (0%) exhibit worse merge rates into our ADV data set. These funds also pose additional identification questions—either outsiders cannot invest or insiders have chosen not to invest in these funds. For these reasons, we focus the remainder of our analysis on funds in the interior of the internal investment distribution: between 1% and 99% inside investment, inclusive (our results are robust to their inclusion). The resulting sample to address known biases in the data is reported inTable 2. Table 2.Summary Statistics: Merged Data Table 2.Summary Statistics: Merged Data Notes.This summary table describes data on the primary data set based on a merged data set of Form ADV and commercial hedge fund data providers (Eureka, HFR, BarclaysHedge, eVestment, and CISDM). Data are taken as of 2016. Data are only taken from funds which are registered as hedge funds; firms must have at least one hedge fund and a minimum level of assets of $20 million. Panel A describes firm level information at the level of the management company. Panel B describes information available at the level of individual funds. Some assets may be double-counted due to the inclusion of fund of funds. Panel B reports additional variables not included inTable 1. Inside Investment corresponds to ownership by management or related parties, the key variable explored in this paper. It is important to note that the exclusion of these funds, particularly the high investment sample, likely biases down our results. Many of the very highest returning funds have high inside investment and cannot be found in commercial hedge fund data sets (Barth et al. 2020). As such, our results therefore represent conservative estimates of the impact of inside investment on fund returns. The legal basis of “related parties” corresponds closely to either personal capital of the GP, or capital exercised by very close affiliates. Specifically, the SEC definesrelated personsas: “Any advisory affiliate and any person that is under common control with your firm.”10In turn, this notion “common control” is commonly interpreted to cover (1) the General Partner, investment manager, and members of management; (2) principal owners of the firm; (3) members of immediate families; and (4) any legal entities under the control of these individuals as a consequence of ownership of stock, voting rights, or other contractual provisions.11 We study the breakdown of “related parties” inTable 3, which illustrates all possible configurations of which parties constitute related parties. The most common response is “Sponsor of GP,” suggesting that the definition of related party most often corresponds to a vehicle used by the actual managers or general partners of the fund. Alternately, related parties can include other closely related entities, such as asset investment by a broker/dealer. A separate set of questions asks the legal name of all related parties: These entities are typically closely related to the management company, share a supervised person three-quarters of the time, and share a common physical office over half of the time. Despite the limitations of this measure in calculating managerial stakes exactly, we document that related parties are typically vehicles for fund investment by the general partners and that they typically represent asset management on behalf of closely related entities that can be considered “inside capital.” Table 3.Related Party Information Table 3.Related Party Information Notes.This table illustrates the identity of related parties, as listed in form ADV in 2016. The rows need not sum to one: firms select as many options as needed to identify all related parties. The inside investment data used in this paper is illustrated inFigure 4. Panel (a) shows the density of responses on inside investment across our full merged data set. Panel (b) shows the distribution of assets under management attributable to inside investment, shown on a log-dollar scale.
article.4. Empirical Strategy: 4.1. Excess Return Measures Throughout our empirical tests, we use theFung and Hsieh (2004)seven-factor model as our benchmark for excess returns: TheFung and Hsieh (2004)factors are widely used in hedge fund research, includingFung et al. (2008)andPatton and Ramdorai (2013). These estimates allow us to compute average excess returns𝛼𝐹⁢𝐻𝑖⁢𝑡αi⁢tF⁢Hfor each month and fund.12 The second return measure we consider is theStyle-Adjusted-Returnmeasure, followingYin (2016): The previous specification subtracts the return earned by a fund for a given period from the average return of all funds of the same style during the same period. This specification is used for ease of calculation from raw returns and for its ability to adjust for the risk associated with different investment strategies. 4.2. Inside Investment and Fund Scalability We test the relationship between excess returns and scalability of the hedge fund, followingYin (2016): In the previous specification, we test two different dependent variables: (i) the return in excess of the style average (followingYin 2016) and (ii) Fung-Hsieh factor models. Our variable of interest is the coefficientβ, from the interaction term betweenlog⁡(𝐴𝑈𝑀𝑖,𝑡−1)log⁡(AUMi,t−1)andHighOwnership𝑖,𝑡−1HighOwnershipi,t−1, an indicator variable taking the value of 1 if the funds insider ownership is greater than the sample median. We control for log(𝐴𝑈𝑀𝑡−1AUMt−1) to take into account the general decreasing returns to scale for all funds. Other controls inXinclude log(total firm𝐴𝑈𝑀𝑡−1AUMt−1), percent AUM𝐹𝑙𝑜𝑤(𝑡−1)→𝑡Flow(t−1)→t, performance fee, management fee, an indicator for a high water mark, log(minimum investment), redemption period, fund age, whether the fund is open to new investment, and leverage. We include fixed effects for the firm, year, and strategy, and cluster at the fund level. All flow and return measures are winsorized at the 1% level. 4.3. Inside Investment and Size To test for the relationship between ownership and size, we also perform a comparable analysis regressing the assets under fund management against the fraction of inside investment: Theψcoefficient here captures the relationship of size and fractional inside investment within firm and year. 4.4. Fund-Flow Sensitivity and Return Predictability Following prior literature, such asChevalier and Ellison (1997), we define fund flows using net flowsritas Using this definition, we also test standard fund-flow sensitivities: In flow specifications, time is measured quarterly. Other controls in𝐗Xinclude leverage, lagged fund size, management fees, performance fees, redemption period, an indicator for high watermark, lagged flows, fund formation, and strategy fixed effects.13The key coefficient of interest isβ1: whether funds with high inside ownership (defined as inside investment above the median) exhibit less flow-performance. Lower flow-performance would indicate when funds with greater inside investment accept less additional funding in response to better prior performance. 4.5. Inside Investment and Performance We next test whether inside investment results in greater risk-adjusted fund-level returns. Accordingly, we adopt a two-step approach. In the first step, we estimate a time-series regression of excess returns on factor exposures, as discussed inSection 4.1. In the second step, we consider three approaches to demonstrate the relationship between inside investment and excess returns. First, we examine a portfolio sort approach in which we create deciles of hedge funds sorted by inside investment. Second, we consider a panel regression which allows us to control for firm, month, and firm by month fixed effects. Third, we consider a standardFama and MacBeth (1973)cross-sectional regression that relates excess returns from fund-specific factors to inside ownership and other variables. A summary of the main dependent variables used in our analysis can be found inTable 4. Table 4.Summary Statistics of Dependent Variables Table 4.Summary Statistics of Dependent Variables Notes.This table presents the summary statistics of key dependent variables used throughout the paper. Each panel summarizes the 10th, 25th, 50th, and 90th percentile, as well as the mean and standard deviation. Years include 2011 through 2016, inclusive. Panel A summarizes the excess return imputed by the Fung-Hsieh model. Panel B summarizes the percent asset flow, computed quarterly, and winsorized at the 1% level. Panel C summarizes the percent of inside investment at the fund level. In this specification, we consider excess returns (𝑟𝑖⁢𝑡−𝑟𝑓⁢𝑡ri⁢t−rf⁢t) to be the net returns after fees minus the risk-free rate, as we take the standpoint of an institutional investor interested in allocating across the broad investable universe of fund managers.14This monthly time-series analysis is run for each ofNfunds to generate fund-specific factor loadings. With the monthly estimates of risk-adjusted returns, we estimate (followingFama and MacBeth 1973) cross-sectional regressions against fund characteristics, including our measure of ownership: The key variable of interest isγ, which captures the predictive role of greater inside investment on excess returns. To measure ownership, we use both the percentage of the fund that consists of insider investment (our preferred measure) as well as the gross insider exposure. This measure of ownership is drawn from annual ADV forms, and it represents the ownership stake from the prior year. Additional controls in𝐗Xinclude controls for fund age, size, and strategy, as well as a firm fixed effect. This means that we compare the excess return performance of two funds, which are both in the same firm, but one of which has higher inside investment. Though standard in the mutual fund literature, a key limitation of theFama and MacBeth (1973)cross-sectional specification is that it does not allow us to control for time fixed effects. To do so, our baseline specification is a panel regression of fund and firm characteristics against excess returns: The fixed effects for adviser include (depending on the specification) firm fixed effects (capturing the effects of moving from one fund in a family to another with greater inside investment), or fund fixed effects (using the time-series variation of inside investment, within the same fund; holding equal all other time-invariant fund characteristics). Therefore, in this specification: our key coefficient of interest,γ, captures the impact of inside investment, either as percentage of fund as well as gross exposure, on excess returnrelativeto another fund in the same family and month with less inside investment. In particular, we include a firm × month fixed effect. This means that we compare the excess return performance of two funds, which are both in the same firm within the same month, but one of which has higher inside investment. This allows us to control for all other invariant firm, fund, and time characteristics which might otherwise drive excess returns. Standard errors are clustered at the month level, followingPetersen (2009).15We also extend our analysis to consider return smoothing motivations, which have been documented for hedge funds, for example, byGetmansky et al. (2004).
article.5. Results: We test the four hypotheses predicted by our framework: first, hedge fund managers allocate their insider capital to their less-scalable strategies; second, insiders restrict access to outside investors in these funds; third, insider funds provide more capital stability; and fourth, insider funds, as a consequence, outperform on a risk-adjusted basis. 5.1. Managers Allocate Insider Capital to Their Less Scalable Strategies We first establish that hedge funds face decreasing returns to scale for funds with high GP capital investments or insider investments. We follow the approach ofYin (2016)by regressing excess returns against asset flows and fund-level ownership. The results inFigure 5estimateEquation (7)for specialized arbitrage strategies. This figure plots the interaction coefficient of lagged assets and high insider funds regressed against style-adjusted returns. We find negative coefficients, which suggests that high-insider funds are less scalable, because higher fund assets predict more negative returns. We find that insider capital is, in fact, allocated in general to funds with more decreasing returns to scale strategies. We also contrast our results across different types of strategies. We find more negative coefficients in categories such as event-driven and relative value, which are typically associated with lower scalability due to liquidity, price impact, and limited float. We find that insider capital is even more tightly matched to decreasing returns to scale strategies in those categories. The interpretation of these results is that when insider funds experience larger assets under management, the excess returns to investors deteriorate more significantly relative to outsider funds. This result holds whether we examine excess returns using a style-adjusted average, as inYin (2016)or Fung-Hsieh factor models. We next establish the strategic aspect of managerial investments in their own funds through analyzing the role of size and inside investment. As a reminder, our framework hypothesizes that a key mechanism driving the superior performance of insider funds is their smaller size, due to decreasing returns to scale in investment technologies. We confirm that insider funds tend to be smaller, in line with our model. InTable 5, we regress the size of the fund against inside investment. In column 2 of Panel A, we focus on our matched data set and find that an additional percent of inside investment is associated with a fund that is smaller by $4–$8 million. This relationship persists when we examine a specification where the dependent variable is the log of assets under management in columns 3 and 4. Table 5.Inside Investment and Fund Size Table 5.Inside Investment and Fund Size Notes.This table shows the panel regression between size and inside investment. Panel A conducts analysis on the matched sample connecting Form ADV with commercial hedge fund data sets (where the key dependent variable is assets under management, taken from the commercial hedge fund data sets, reported as the log of AUM or in millions). Panel B performs analysis on the complete ADV data set, usingGross Asset Valueas the dependent variable. All specifications regress the fraction of the fund which consists on insider investment against a measure of size, measured yearly. Across all specifications, columns 1 and 3 perform this regression with no additional controls; columns 2 and 4 add firm and year fixed effects. Standard errors are in parentheses. *p< 0.1; **p< 0.05; ***p< 0.01. To address concerns about biases in the merged data set, we retest this specification on the Form ADV data set only and present the results inTable 5, Panel B. These specifications use the field “Gross Asset Value” derived from fund-level information in Form ADV. Gross asset value differs from assets under management in that it does not subtract out the value of short positions from the portfolio, and therefore it overestimates true fund size. Despite the limitations of this measure, using this field as a dependent variable enables us to avoid losing observations on the merge between our Form ADV data set and the commercial hedge fund data sets. The results are very similar when not restricting on funds that merge into commercial hedge fund data sets: We find in column 2 that within a firm, funds with an additional percent of inside investment are around $10 million smaller in gross asset value. These results provide additional support for our main hypothesis: inside investment funds are smaller, suggesting that managers are less likely to hit the limits of the capacity constraints of their investment strategy when their own private capital is deployed. We examine other possible determinants of inside investment, reporting the results in Table A.1 in the Online Appendix. In this specification, we run a yearly panel regression of inside investment against a variety of fund and firm characteristics. We begin in column 1 with a regression of the percentage of fund assets attributable to investments by insiders and related parties against a number of fund and firm characteristics without additional fixed effects. Standard errors are clustered at the firm level. We then add fixed effects for fund inception year, firm, and year of observation. The most robust correlate of inside investment remains fund size: high inside investment funds are smaller. We also find that leverage is negatively associated with inside investment once we control for firm fixed effects. One interpretation of the risk explanation is that skilled managers facing capacity constraints may simply prefer to avoid all outside money (both debt and equity) in select funds, in which they prioritize allocations of their own private capital. For this reason, they may simply prefer to avoid accepting additional assets under management as reflecting debt claims. In addition, inside investment may act to insulate funds from capital flows. Consequently, funds with low inside investment may need to rely on credit lines, or leverage, to manage shocks to liquidity instead.16 There is weak evidence that fees—particularly performance fees—are associated with higher inside investment, though this relationship is generally small in magnitude and not statistically significant once we add firm fixed effects. Although our basic approach assumes that managers and investors take fees as given, one possible reason to consider the endogenous determination of fees might be that insiders use higher fees to further ration out investors in funds with greater levels of inside investment. However, the key predictions of our framework—that inside investment will be associated with higher postfee alpha—remains as long as we assume that managers cannot fully set fees to maximize all of the investor surplus. Fees appear to be sticky, do not exhibit substantial cross-sectional or time series variation, and are not robustly associated with inside investment once we control for other variables.17 5.2. Insiders Actively Restrict Access to Outside Capital Our second key point of supporting evidence examines the relationship between flows and performance directly. We test whether GP capital is allocated into less-scalable funds and will be most valuable when managers are able to also restrict outside capital entry into these funds. We begin by considering consider how lagged excess returns relate to asset flows to funds. Figure 6plots a nonparametric relationship between lagged returns and fund inflows by funds with a greater or lesser degree of insider investment measured at a quarterly frequency. Insider funds are defined as those with a greater-than sample average amount of fraction of fund assets attributable to insiders.18 For outside investors, we document a complicated relationship between flows and performance. This relationship is convex for low levels of past returns, hits a peak for higher levels of past returns, and then becomes concave. Overall, this pattern suggests a combination of increasing returns to scale that then become decreasing with higher levels of past returns—consistent with the patterns observed inLiang et al. (2020), which highlights the importance of share restrictions in influencing the relationship between flows and performance. Our specification instead focuses on the distinction between outsider fund flows in comparison with the flows for inside investment funds. In this section, we first focus on the flow-performance relationship after positive returns and then consider the impact of negative returns in the next section. The fact that high inside-investment funds do not attract higher inflows is strong evidence in our setting against signaling-based explanations of the role of inside investment. If personal stakes by managers were necessary to attract additional capital, we would expect that funds with greater inside investment might attract additional funds—particularly when prior returns were high. Instead, we find that high-inside-investment funds do not scale up as much as outsider funds when returns are high. These results point to a role for moral hazard explanations for why inside investment is associated with superior performance. Table 6presents additional flow performance specifications to test possible signaling explanations, as outlined inEquation (10). The dependent variable in these specifications is the percent flow or an indicator whether inflows are positive. A variety of other controls are included in these specifications.19We find in column 1 that greater lagged excess returns predict lower flows among funds with greater inside investment, suggesting that insider funds do not accept as much flow in response to positive returns. Table 6.Fund Flow Analysis Table 6.Fund Flow Analysis Notes.This table summarizes the relationship between fund flows and quarterly excess returns. Columns 1 and 2 relate excess returns to fund flows of the same funds. Column 1 uses lagged excess returns as a continuous variable, while column 2 evaluates past excess returns as a dummy variable for whether they fall below the 10th percentile of returns. Columns 3 through 6 relate excess returns to fund flows of funds within the same family, referred to as complementary funds. Columns 5 and 6 restrict their analysis to funds where the inside investment is greater than the mean level. Firm, inception year, calendar year, and strategy fixed effects are included in all regressions. All regressions also include controls for leverage, management fees, performance fee, redemption days, high watermark, lagged log gross asset value, and lagged flow (winsorized). *p< 0.1; **p< 0.05; ***p< 0.01. Although these results provide strong evidence for the role of insider capital and flows in predicting returns, a chief concern with our interpretation is the role of public signaling. Fund managers, particularly for less-established funds, may need to use inside capital commitments to convince outside investors of fund quality. When managers are required to hold costly private stakes to demonstrate quality and earn management fees on outside capital, inside investment could potentially predict flows and returns. We address these concerns inTable 6by examiningotherfunds within the family. We do so to test a broader class of signaling explanations—perhaps funds operate certain “flagship” funds to an optimum scale to advertise returns (Fung et al. 2020). If this is true, we might expect that returns do not predict flows for some high insider funds (because managers are using these funds to advertise high returns)—but signaling stories would predict that foregoing additional fee income today (to produce better returns for marketing) should be followed up with higher inflows in the future, perhaps in other funds, generating future fee income. We find little evidence that signaling concerns drive our results. In columns 3 and 4 ofTable 6, we find lagged excess returns do not drive flows to other funds in the family, measured at either the extensive or the intensive margin (whether inflows are positive). In columns 5 and 6, we restrict our attention to high ownership funds. For this subset, as well, we find little evidence that high returns drive inflows to other funds in our family. Investors appear to distinguish between different funds in the family when allocating capital, and to preferentially increase flows only to the funds that directly outperform. These results provide evidence against the idea that managers may want to leave personal capital in some funds in order to signal their quality, thus driving flows (and management fees) to other vehicles they manage. While this finding does not fully rule out all possible signaling explanation for our results, the bulk of the evidence is more consistent with a moral hazard view. To further explore the role of active capital rationing, we next focus on a sample of funds for which we are able to establish the role of managerial discretion in accepting capital. InTable 7, we examine funds which are explicitly closed to outside investors as reported by commercial databases. In columns 3–6 of this table, we find strong evidence that funds that are closed to outside investors strongly outperform on a factor-adjusted basis. In these specifications, we regress a dummy variable for funds open to investors against excess returns measured using the Fung-Hsieh model, finding that funds closed to outside investors outperform by 0.23% each month, corresponding to 2.8% a year in excess returns. Table 7.Open for New Investments Table 7.Open for New Investments Notes.This table shows the panel regressions between fund and whether they are open for new investments. The analysis is based on the HFR, Eurekahedge, and BarclayHedge data sets. Columns 1 and 2 relate percent of inside investment to the openness of a fund. Columns 3 and 4 relate the openness of the fund to monthly excess returns, as measured by the Fung-Hsieh Seven-Factors model. *p< 0.1; **p< 0.05; ***p< 0.01. This finding also argues against a possible concern of reverse causality—insider funds could be smaller and less scalable because of outsider preferences, rather than insider preferences. Insider funds may simply have more difficulty raising outside capital, rather reflecting a preference of insider capital. Instead, we find that insider funds disproportionatelyrestrictoutsider capital entry entirely, and correspondingly have much higher returns. These findings, instead, suggest that insider funds are desirable from the standpoint of outsider capital, and their entry in these funds is rationed and restricted by managers. Funds which do not accept additional inflows are able to generate superior performance. These excess returns suggest that outsiders would like to enter these funds and are being actively rationed out of them. The presence of these funds, however, poses a challenge for traditional views of delegated asset managers—why do managers forgo management fees by not accepting outside investors into these funds? A potential resolution to this puzzle is suggested in columns 1 and 2 ofTable 7. In these specifications, we examine the relationship between inside investment and funds that are open to investors. We find that funds that are closed to investors are substantially more likely to have a greater concentration of inside investment. These results highlights managerial capital rationing as a mechanism to explain the outperformance of insider funds. Managers restrict the participation of outsider investors both on the intensive margin, but restricting inflows to insider funds that outperform, and on the extensive margin, by prohibiting outsider capital entry entirely on some highly outperforming funds. The superior performance of these insider funds does not drive inflows to other funds within the family, which points to the role of moral hazard as a key driver of the relationship between inside investment and fund returns. 5.3. Insider Funds Provide More Capital Stability To examine the possible capital stability benefits of inside investment, we return toFigure 6, but instead focus on the flow performance in response to negative returns on theleftside of the graph. In this region, we observe that funds with large amounts of outside investment experience substantial capital flight in response to poor returns. This corresponds to the argument inChen et al. (2010)that capital flight by outside investors resembles a “run” on funds. By contrast, we observe that capital flows are strongly attenuated among hedge funds with substantial amounts of inside investment. This illustrates one of the core benefits of inside investment in our framework: that inside investment is more stable than outside investment. These results are very complementary to past results focusing on share restrictions and suggest that the composition of the investor pool can provide similar benefits as contractual restrictions on capital mobility. They are also similar to other past work (Chen et al. 2010) in mutual funds that argues that large investors also attenuate the link between performance and flows. Our work suggests that inside investors can play a similar role to large investors in providing these insurance benefits to firms. We incorporate these insights, in our model, by giving fund managers an additional disutility from managing outside capital, which is a reduced-form parameter to capture the greater insurance provided by inside investors. We further explore the capital insurance benefits of insider funds in a regression inTable 6. Column 2 of this table examines capital flows in response to excess returns that are below the 10th percentile of the excess return distribution, that is, the realization of left tail risks. For all funds, we find that such extreme events lead to substantial capital outflow. By contrast, high inside investment funds see relative positiveinflowin response to such extreme negative shocks. This capital stabilization benefit is economically strong, enough to overcome the negative impact of such negative return shocks, and is also statistically significant. These results demonstrate that inside investment differs from outside investment in its stability. In turn, these capital stability (or insurance) benefits of inside investment may enable funds to engage in alpha generation strategies in the first place that come with some tail risks. 5.4. Insider Funds Outperform Outside Funds The analysis thus far has analyzed how managers allocate private capital to funds with more decreasing returns to scale and optimize the size of such funds. In this section, we build on this analysis to explore the implications of this capital scaling on the returns experienced by outside investors. Our framework predicts that within a firm, funds with a greater proportion of inside capital will outperform, because managers will allocate their least-scalable strategies to funds managing their money, and further internalize the capacity constraints of the investment strategy when accepting new capital. Funds with greater inside capital retain greater alpha, in equilibrium, because managers maximize profits by not accepting additional outside capital beyond the capacity limit of the investing strategy. We proceed with this analysis in two steps. First, we use a trading strategy approach which divides funds into portfolios sorted by inside investment. Second, we consider a panel regression approach. InFigure 7, we show the raw returns on portfolios, sorted yearly into insider investment deciles. The resulting returns, as a consequence, correspond to an implementable trading strategy that invests across funds. An investor who gains access to funds in the top decile of inside investment realizes substantially higher raw performance compared with an investor who has access only the bottom decile of funds sorted on inside investment (roughly, the difference between a 35% cumulative gain and only a 12% cumulative gain across the period 2011–2016Q2).Figure 8shows that this difference persists when we compare high and low insider investmentwithinfirm as well, as the spread difference (correcting for Fung-Hsieh factor exposure) between portfolios persists over time. We also examine the tradable strategy approach in a regression context, across the 10 decile portfolios, which allows us to control for factor exposure as well inTable 8. The first key attribute we emphasize is that average follows a clear relationship to portfolio returns. The decile with the least amount of inside investment returns 17 basis points of returns monthly, whereas the highest decile returns 42 basis points of abnormal returns. Statistical power is a challenge in these specifications, because we are simply averaging returns into portfolios over our sample period. For these reasons, our panel-based approach at the fund level is most precise for precise calculation of excess returns. However, we still find that the top three deciles produce average returns statistically significant for zero, which are also quite economically large. The long-short portfolio, which invests in high inside investment funds and shorts low inside investment returns, produces 300 basis points (3%) annually in return. This economically significant measure suggests that inside investment is a powerful predictor of fund performance, complementing our main regressions through a trading strategy. Table 8.Risk and Fund Characteristics by Inside Investment-Sorted Portfolios Table 8.Risk and Fund Characteristics by Inside Investment-Sorted Portfolios Notes.This table reports averages for the 10 decile portfolios formed based on the level of inside investment for each fund. The portfolios are formed each year and correspond toFigure 7. The first 10 rows correspond to each portfolio, sorted by least inside investment to greatest inside investment. The last row computeststatistics based on the difference between the highest and lowest decile portfolio. Columns 2–11 show (1) the formed portfolio’s average monthly return, (2) the management fee in percent, (3) the performance fee in percent, (4) the advance notice in days, (5) the redemption period in days, (6) the total redemption period in days, (7) reported leverage, (8) a measure of value at risk (VaR), (9) the fund’s expected shortfall, and (10) the liquidity measure fromGetmansky et al. (2004). We also find interesting patterns of fund characteristics in the other columns ofTable 8. Performance fees are slightly higher. At the same time, high inside investment funds appear to havefewershare restrictions as defined by the advance notice period or the redemption notice period. These two factors mean that high inside investment funds require, in total, about 15 fewer days of redemption notice compared with low inside investment funds. This fact highlights that our results on high inside investment funds cannot be driven only by share restrictions—because these funds actually have less share restrictions. They also do not appear to be driven entirely by liquidity—the high inside investment funds actually appear to have higher liquidity as measured by return autocorrelations as in theGetmansky et al. (2004)measure. Last, we consider the role of risk—whether high insider investment funds have more risk (as measured by VaR or expected shortfall). Here, we find very meaningful estimates which have strongly reshaped our interpretation of our key results. We find that high inside investment funds have substantially higher Value at Risk (VaR) and a higher expected shortfall. Our estimates are quite large—The top decile has 0.83 higher VaR and 1.19 higher expected shortfall. We interpret these higher risk performance characteristics, in the context of higher abnormal returns but lower share restrictions, in the context of a capital preservation story. Intuitively—because inside capital is more patient, funds are able to use this capital to pursue arbitrage strategies that generate abnormal returns but may also generate some tail risk. This result is very complementary to our initial proposed mechanism because such arbitrage strategies are also likely to have important scale limitations. Inside investment, as a consequence, may be beneficial for fund returns both because it incentivizes operating strategy at the right scale and also because it contributes to capital preservation that enables funds to realize these returns even if they generate some tail risk. We next turn to the panel regression, where we have more power due to the consideration of all funds and more granular fund-level controls for factor exposure. These factors allow us to combine the evidence we have on higher returns and potentially more tail risk to provide a composite estimate of excess returns. In our main specifications, we use a two-step approach to control more closely for fund factor exposures. We analyze the role of inside investment on risk-adjusted returns. Panel A ofTable 9presents a panel specification, using as the key regressor the percent of a fund’s asset under management that can be attributed to insider investment in the prior year against excess returns. In columns 1–4, we measure excess returns using a seven-factor model in the first stage, and we show the results of a second-stage regression of inside investment against excess returns.20 Table 9.Relationship Between Inside Investment and Excess Return Table 9.Relationship Between Inside Investment and Excess Return Notes.This table shows the panel regression between the excess monthly return of an investment advisor and insider investment. Panel A measures inside investment as a percentage of fund assets, while Panel B uses the log of gross inside investment. A size control is always included. Specifications (1) includes only size and three lags of excess return. Specifications (2) adds firm fixed effects, month fixed effects, and fund level controls (age of fund inception and strategy type). Specification (3) includes size, three lags of excess return, and a fund fixed effect. Specification (4) adds a firm × month fixed effect. Standard errors are clustered monthly. Specifications (1) in this table shows impact of lagged inside investment on excess returns, including a control only for size. Specification (2) adds controls for fund characteristics (fund inception year and strategy) and a firm fixed effect. This ensured we are comparing two funds within the same fund family. Both sets of specifications suggest large positive and statistically significant effects of inside investment on fund performance. Column (3) replace the fund controls and the firm fixed effect with afundfixed effect. This controls for all fund-invariant characteristics, using the time series variation in inside investment within funds. Column (4) additionally adds a firm × month fixed effect to remove all time and firm varying characteristics. We compare, as a consequence, two funds within the same firm family and month, which differ in the amount of inside investment. Our estimates in that column suggest that a fund with a 1% increase in inside investment experiences a 0.33-basis-point higher excess return per month. Scaling our results, we find a 0.9-percentage-point increase in alpha per year for a fund with a standard deviation increase (22%) in the amount of inside investment relative to another fund in the same firm with similar characteristics in the same month. These results are both economically and statistically significant and suggest a strong importance for internal investment as a predictor of cross-sectional fund performance. The larger magnitude and significance of results when controlling for both firm and fund fixed effects suggests the importance of discretionary fund allocation by insiders: There is high dispersion of fund returns within firms in our sample, and insiders choose which investment strategies to pursue for which funds, as well as which funds to invest in. Our results suggest that inside capital is more likely to be deployed in funds that outperform others within the family. We also find a strong role for inside investment in Panel B ofTable 9, in which we examine thegrossamount of inside investment, rather than the fractional amount, while also controlling for size and other fund-level characteristics. These results make a series of sample restrictions outlined in the previous section. However, in line with prior analyses, we also examine a Fama-MacBeth cross-sectional regression in Table A.2 in the Online Appendix. In this estimate, as outlined previously, we do not control for year fixed effects, although we add firm fixed effects in column 4. However, we also find comparable results in these specifications across the seven-factor model, illustrating the robustness of our result: that greater inside investment is associated with superior performance. 5.5. Strategic Steering of Investors into Lower-Performing Funds The results from the previous section provide strong evidence of a role for insider investment in driving fund returns. This raises the possibility that investments by insiders are a critical component of the earnings for managers, alongside management and incentive fees. Furthermore, it is possible that fund managers may seek to further take advantage of this relationship by steering clients into lower-performing funds. We conducts an event study in the aftermath of the creation of a new fund among firms that previously only had one fund.21The creation of an additional fund presents two possibilities for fund managers: (i) they can either keep their internal capital invested in the original fund, using the new fund to attract new capital, or (ii) they can shift their own capital to the new fund, and market the original fund to investors. If the amount of insider capital is an important determinant of fund performance, we expect different fund performance in theoriginalfund under the two cases. If managers are shifting their capital outside of the fund, we expect the performance of the original fund to deteriorate because managers are no longer as invested in success of the fund. If, conversely, managers keep their capital in the original fund, the performance of the original fund should remain strong. To fix ideas, consider the case of Citadel and the creation of the fund vehicle—tactical trading—that was spun out of the main flagship fund to specialize in high-frequency trading in 2007. This fund vehicle was seeded with high amounts of inside investment and subsequently performed extremely well over the subsequent decade. Although this case represents just one example, it provides a possible illustrative case for some motives on why fund managers may decide to create new fund vehicles and shift inside investment within funds under their control.22 To test this possibility in the data, we focus on all cases in which a hedge fund that previously only operated one fund opens a new second fund. We isolate two cases: one in which the new fund has less internal investment than the original, the original fund has “high inside investment,” and another in which the new fund has more internal investment than the original (“low inside investment”). We plot cumulative returns of the original fund for the two-year window both before and after the fund creation date. We track the returns on the original fund to avoid the issue of incubation bias (Evans 2010) that would be posed by analyzing the returns of the newly generated fund. As shown inFigure 9, our results suggest that fund performance is relatively similar before the event date for the original fund, regardless of whether the firm subsequently creates a new fund with high or low internal investment. Differences grow more pronounced in the aftermath of fund creation. We find that when the original fund has “high inside investment”—suggesting that managers keep their internal capital in the original fund—fund performance improves when the newly formed fund contains that insider capital instead. We expect to see this difference because managers are more invested in the success of the initial fund if their capital remains deployed in the fund. If their own capital has moved to a different fund, performance tends to suffer in the window after fund creation.23 Although these results are not fully conclusive, they are suggestive of the possibility of “skimming” motives on the part of fund managers. If managers are able to shift their internal investments across funds within the same family, they seem to be able to focus their investments on successful funds while steering outside capital into the lower-performing funds. These results provide additional context to our discussion of mechanisms inSection 2and previous empirical results suggesting that active decisions made by fund managers regarding fund creation and capital deployment play a role in determining returns for outside investors. To be clear, this analysis does not identify whether this is due to insiders having better information on which fund managers can outperform relative to outsiders or because managers devote more effort when greater amounts of personal capital are on the line. Despite the multiple possible explanations, we emphasize that our result provides novel evidence on the role of inside investment in shaping fund performance as new funds are created.
article.6. Testing Alternative Hypotheses: The previous results are subject to several important caveats. We are not able to fully control for whether our results are driven by some element of risk or instead result from agency conflicts within the firm. Despite our attempts to control for risk using the benchmark fund factors, it is also possible that the outperformance of high inside-investment funds occurs due to a novel risk factor. To further analyze the mechanisms driving our main result, we examine fund decisions along other dimensions to address alternate hypotheses. We explore these points of robustness inTable 10. Each row of this table reflects a different regression that alters our baseline specification, shown in row 1. We show the coefficient on inside investment against Fung-Hsieh excess returns, controlling for only lagged returns and size (column 1), fund fixed effects (column 2), and firm fixed effects (column 3). Subsequent panels correspond to alternate risks, additional controls, and alternate measures of inside investment. Table 10.Robustness of Relationship Between Insider Investment and Excess Returns Table 10.Robustness of Relationship Between Insider Investment and Excess Returns Notes.This table shows a robustness ofTable 9. The first equation reproduces the baseline specification: the relationship between inside investment and Fung-Hsieh excess returns, controlling for lagged returns, and successively introducing fund level controls, month fixed effects, firm fixed effects, fund fixed effects, and firm × month fixed effects (column 4). Additional panels account for alternative risk measurements, additional controls, and alternate measures of inside investment. Each row reflects a separate regression, which controls for an additional risk factor or on a separate sample, relative to our baseline specification. 6.1. Controlling for Additional Risks One alternative hypothesis to our analysis is that superior performance in insider funds may reflect uncontrolled risk exposures. This might happen if, for instance, insiders use private funds to engage in specialized strategies that generate specific kinds of risk unsuited for broad sets of limited partners. Indeed, part of our main explanation is that the capital stability benefits of inside investment may facilitate this kind of tail risk. If these risks are not accounted for in our asset pricing models, we will fail to account for the hidden risk associated with the abnormally high equity returns achieved in insider portfolios. Panel B ofTable 10accounts for four kinds of alternative risk. Row (2) adjusts for macroeconomic risk using theBali et al. (2014)risk measure. Specifically, we include additional macroeconomic uncertainty variables in our factor model in addition to the Fung-Hsieh factors. Row (3) adjusts for systematic risk using theBali et al. (2012)approach. We also control directly for VaR (row (3)) and expected shortfall (row (5)). We observe very similar results across all model specifications after accounting for these additional risk factors, suggesting that inside investment remains an economically strong and statistically significant predictor of returns even accounting for alternative risks. 6.2. Incorporating Additional Controls We next account for additional controls not considered in our baseline model. In row (6) ofTable 10, we explore the robustness of our measure of inside investment and theGetmansky et al. (2004)liquidity measure. This approach uses an indication of autocorrelation in returns to estimate the extent to which return series are smoothed, hence providing a measure of asset liquidity, with higher numbers indicate greater asset liquidity. We find that our results are quite similar controlling for this liquidity measure. In row (7), we control for theOzik and Sadka (2015)governance score. Because inside investment is closely associated with governance, a natural concern is that inside investment may simply proxy for other contractural provisions contained in this measure or through controls for advance notice and redemption period (row 8). Our results are quite similar controlling for either measure of these contractual limitations on capital withdrawal. Another natural concern comes from the nature of commercial hedge fund data set reporting. To account for backfill bias, we adopt theJorion and Schwarz (2019)suggestion to correct for fund numerical sequence as a measure of when funds were added to databases. If anything, our results appear stronger (using fund or firm fixed effects) after this backfill correction. 6.3. Measuring Alternative Measures of Inside Investment Next, we account for alternate measures of inside investment. First, we restrict our attentiononlyto inside investment that is focusing on the subset of funds with only GP investments as their related party inTable 10, row (10). We observe very similar results in this specification. We also consider the managerial delta ofAgarwal et al. (2009)in two ways. We impute a “mechanical” component of personal capital contributions derived from rolling over prior fee income from the observed inside investment, attributing the residual as reflective of managers’ discretionary capital contributions. We then first directly include the ADN variable as a control in our regression (row (11)). Second, we also adjust our inside investment variable to remove mechanically reinvested fees (row 12). What remains reflects discretionary decisions to invest personal capital, and we find that this residual component, in both specifications, is substantially larger than our baseline specifications. These results suggest that discretion in personal capital investments reflects an independent and quite important determinant of excess returns above and beyond the existing ADN measure. We also separate out young funds specifically (row (13)) to account for the possibility that newer fund vehicles may simply have not had enough time to accumulate personal capital resulting from prior accumulated fees. The fact that we find that personal capital appears to matter even for young funds points to the role again of strategic capital allocation, rather than reflecting simply mechanically accumulated fees. Finally, we also relax our assumption that we only consider the interior of the inside investment distribution [1, 99]. In row (14), we also include funds with 0 and 100% inside investment. Our results are even higher on this subsample. We also examine whether a fund stops reporting to the database, which is another proxy for fund failure (although fund closures can reflect either purely negative failures, as well as fund closures for other reasons). This draws on the discussion inGetmansky (2012), who uses hedge fund liquidation as an outcome variable. InFigure 10, we report a Kaplan-Meier survival curve, comparing funds above and below the median in insider investment. We observe that insider funds have a slightly higher survival rate (alternatively, a slightly lower failure rate). Although we are not able to purely interpret failure as only reflecting fund closure, we emphasize that this analysis also illustrates the capital stability benefits of inside investment: as high inside investment funds appear to be better protected against fund failure. 6.4. Heterogenous Treatment Effects As suggested byLiang et al. (2020), investment styles face different capacity constraints. If capacity constraints are driving our main effect, we would also expect a heterogenous treatment effect across different investment styles. Specifically, investment styles that face larger capacity constraint frictions would have a stronger relationship between inside investments and excess returns. Testing the relationship between inside investment and excess returns, we bucket by investment styles and present the results inFigure 11, as in column 2 of Panel ATable 9. Panel A of this figure plots the coefficient of inside investment against excess return by fund categories, as measured in our set of commercial hedge fund data sets. The main effects are driven by funds that engage in specialist absolute return strategies, arbitrage strategies, and equity funds. Within equity funds, in Panel B, the effects are driven by long–short funds. These fund strategies also plausibly feature capacity constraints in their investment strategies. By contrast, effects are insignificant among fund-of-funds and Commodity Trading Advisors, which are typically associated with greater capacity. We additionally test our main result across the distribution of inside investment. As shown by the quantile regression in Figure B.7 in the online appendix, our results are largely being driven by funds with higher levels of inside investment: those funds for which inside capital provides a substantial component of the capital base, for which we expect to see the highest-powered incentives.24In Table A.3 in the Online Appendix, we examine our basic regression across different fund size levels, finding significant effects for the top two fund size quantiles, corresponding to fund sizes of at least $126 million.25 6.5. Ruling out Market Timing of Fund Managers An alternative hypothesis in explaining our main result—that greater insider investment predicts higher excess returns—may be that managers have superior private information about the abilities of funds than do outside investors and therefore time the market by deploying personal capital to these funds. Superior information is plausible for hedge fund managers who understand internal strategies and managerial ability of different fund managers at a higher level than outsider investors. To explore this hypothesis, we estimate the following specification inTable 11: Table 11.Fund Flows and Performance Table 11.Fund Flows and Performance Notes.This table shows the panel regression between size and flows by insiders and outsiders. “Insider Flow” corresponds to changes in capital provided by insiders and related parties, while “Outsider Flow” captures changes in capital provision by all other investors. Changes in both types of flow are measured annually with the release of new ADV forms. Column 2 adds year fixed effects, and column 3 adds firm fixed effects. Standard errors are clustered at the year level. *p< 0.1; **p< 0.05; ***p< 0.01. This specification tests whetherchangesin insider investment predict excess returns. Our test finds that changes do not predict excess returns in either inside or outside flows. Although this test is not fully conclusive regarding the channel of superior inside information, this result suggests that insiders do not appear to be able to time their capital allocation decisions in ways that predicts future excess returns. Put differently, levels of inside investment, rather than changes in investment, predict future returns.
